The '''Bahrain national football team''' ({{langx|ar|منتخب الْبَحرَيْن لِكُرَّةُ الْقَدَم}}) represents [[Bahrain]] in international [[association football|football]] and is controlled by the [[Bahrain Football Association]], which was founded in 1951. The country is affiliated with [[FIFA]] since 1968 and has been a member of the [[Asian Football Confederation|AFC]] since 1957. They have never reached the FIFA World Cup.

Bahrain won the FIFA's most improved team award in 2004, and finished fourth at the [[2004 AFC Asian Cup]], beating [[Uzbekistan national football team|Uzbekistan]] in the quarter-finals but losing to [[Japan national football team|Japan]] in the semi-finals 4–3. Bahrain then lost to [[Iran national football team|Iran]] in the third-place match, thus finishing in fourth place overall. Bahrain had a golden year in 2019, winning both the [[WAFF Championship]] and the [[Arabian Gulf Cup]] for the first time, under the stewardship of [[Hélio Sousa]]. On 4 January 2025, Bahrain won the 26th Arabian Gulf Cup, defeating [[Oman national football team|Oman]] 2–1.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Duerden |first1=John |title=5 things we learned from 26th Gulf Cup in Kuwait |url=https://www.arabnews.com/node/2585320/sport |website=Arab News |date=5 January 2025 |access-date=4 August 2025}}</ref>

== History ==
[[File:Kamaluddin in First National Soccer Team.jpg|thumb|The 1959 national football team]]

=== Early years ===
Although Bahrain’s first national football team was established in 1959, the side was not officially organized until 1966, when it played a friendly match against [[Kuwait national football team|Kuwait]] that ended in a 4–4 draw. During this period, Bahrain, despite remaining under British influence, had been granted a degree of autonomy that allowed the country to develop its football infrastructure. Historically, Bahrain was considered one of the weaker teams in the Gulf region, which included stronger sides such as [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]], [[Qatar national football team|Qatar]], the [[United Arab Emirates national football team|United Arab Emirates]], and [[Kuwait national football team|Kuwait]]. As a result, Bahrain’s early international participation was largely limited to the [[Arabian Gulf Cup]].

Bahrain qualified for its first [[AFC Asian Cup]] in [[1988 AFC Asian Cup|1988]], but finished at the bottom of its group with two draws. In the years that followed, the senior national team received limited attention and investment, despite notable achievements at youth levels, particularly with the [[Bahrain national under-17 football team|under-17]] and [[Bahrain national under-20 football team|under-20]] teams. It was not until the late 20th century that Bahrain began to show significant improvement, marking a turning point in the development of football in the country.

Bahrain delivered notable performances during the qualification campaigns for the [[2000 AFC Asian Cup]] and the [[2002 FIFA World Cup]], reaching the final round of World Cup qualifying for the first time. Although the team did not qualify in either competition, Bahrain recorded significant victories over [[Iran national football team|Iran]]—winning 1–0 in [[Aleppo]] during the [[2000 AFC Asian Cup qualification|2000 Asian Cup qualifiers]] and 3–1 at home during the [[2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2002 World Cup qualifiers]]. The latter result had implications for the regional qualification standings, as it prevented Iran from securing a direct World Cup berth and contributed to [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]]’s qualification. The match subsequently drew attention due to celebrations by some Bahraini supporters, which added to existing tensions between the two countries’ football fan bases.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.teammelli.com/2015/01/10/history-of-iran-vs-bahrain/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150115112017/http://www.teammelli.com/2015/01/10/history-of-iran-vs-bahrain |archive-date=15 January 2015 |title=History of Iran vs. Bahrain {{!}} TeamMelli}}</ref>

=== 2004 AFC Asian Cup ===
Bahrain delivered a strong performance at the [[2004 AFC Asian Cup]] in only its second appearance in the tournament. Drawn into a group with hosts [[China national football team|China]], [[Qatar national football team|Qatar]], and [[Indonesia national football team|Indonesia]], the team progressed through the group stage undefeated, earning a 2–2 draw against China in [[Beijing]], a 1–1 draw with Qatar, and a 3–1 victory over Indonesia to advance to the quarter-finals for the first time. Bahrain then defeated [[Uzbekistan national football team|Uzbekistan]] on penalties after a 2–2 draw in the quarter-finals. In the semi-finals, Bahrain narrowly lost 4–3 after extra time to defending champions [[Japan national football team|Japan]], before falling 4–2 to [[Iran national football team|Iran]] in the third-place match. The team’s performance in the tournament is regarded as a significant milestone in the development of Bahraini football.

=== 2006 World Cup ===
After both [[Uzbekistan national football team|Uzbekistan]] and Bahrain finished third in their respective groups during the [[2006 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round|2006 FIFA World Cup qualifiers]], the two teams met in a two-legged AFC playoff. Bahrain advanced on the away goals rule after a 1–1 aggregate draw. This result qualified Bahrain for an inter-confederation playoff against the fourth-placed [[CONCACAF]] team, [[Trinidad and Tobago national football team|Trinidad and Tobago]], for a place at the World Cup. The first leg ended 1–1 in [[Port of Spain]], but Bahrain lost the return match 1–0 in [[Manama]], allowing Trinidad and Tobago to qualify for the World Cup for the first time.

=== 2007 Asian Cup ===
Bahrain competed in [[2007 AFC Asian Cup qualification#Group D|Group D]] during the [[2007 AFC Asian Cup qualification|qualification]] stage for the [[2007 AFC Asian Cup]]. The team fielded a largely [[Bahrain national under-23 football team|under-23]] squad in its match against [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]], which ended in a 2–0 defeat. Bahrain ultimately secured qualification by defeating [[Kuwait national football team|Kuwait]] in their final group match. At the tournament, Bahrain were eliminated in the group stage after losses to [[Indonesia national football team|Indonesia]] and [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]], despite recording an upset victory over the [[South Korea national football team|Korea Republic]].

=== 2010 World Cup ===
[[File:SocceroosvsBahrain.jpg|thumb|200px|The Bahrain national football team playing [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]] on 10 June 2009 in a World Cup qualifier]]
In the [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round|third round]] of the [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2010 FIFA World Cup qualification]] campaign, Bahrain were placed in [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round#Group 2|Group B]] alongside [[Japan national football team|Japan]], [[Oman national football team|Oman]], and [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]], finishing second to advance to the final round. In the final group stage, Bahrain placed third behind [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]] and [[Japan national football team|Japan]], but ahead of [[Uzbekistan national football team|Uzbekistan]] and [[Qatar national football team|Qatar]]. This set up a two-legged playoff against [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]] to determine Asia’s fifth-placed team. After a 0–0 draw in the home leg, Bahrain earned a 2–2 draw in Riyadh with a stoppage-time goal, progressing on the away goals rule.

Bahrain then faced [[New Zealand men's national football team|New Zealand]] in the inter-confederation playoff, where a win would have secured their first World Cup appearance. The first leg in Manama ended goalless on 10 October 2009, and Bahrain were defeated 1–0 in the return leg in Wellington on 14 November 2009, narrowly missing out on qualification for a second consecutive cycle.

=== Crisis period ===
==== 2011 Asian Cup ====
Bahrain qualified for the [[2011 AFC Asian Cup]], hosted by neighbouring Qatar, and were placed in a group with [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]], [[South Korea national football team|South Korea]], and [[India men's national football team|India]]. In their opening match, Bahrain faced South Korea in an attempt to replicate their 2–1 victory from the 2007 tournament, but South Korea came from behind to win by the same scoreline. Bahrain then kept their qualification hopes alive with a 5–2 win over India. However, a 1–0 defeat to Australia in their final group match resulted in Bahrain’s elimination at the group stage.

==== 2014 World Cup ====
{{see also|Bahrain 10–0 Indonesia}}
In the [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2014 FIFA World Cup qualifiers]], Bahrain did considerably worse. In the [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round|third round]], they were drawn against [[Indonesia national football team|Indonesia]], [[Iran national football team|Iran]] and [[Qatar national football team|Qatar]]. Although they managed to defeat Indonesia both home and away, they also lost 6–0 by Iran away from home, and drew their other 3 games. Although they had a higher goal difference than Qatar, they needed an extra point to advance to the next round, or Qatar had to be beaten by Iran in the final round. If they had also drawn to Iran away from home, they would have advanced. But neither luck came to them, and their campaign ended in the third round, their worst result since the [[1998 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|1998 World Cup qualifiers]].
{{:2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round|transcludesection=Group E|showteam=BHR}}

==== 2015 Asian Cup ====
The [[2015 AFC Asian Cup]] once again became a disappointment for the Bahrainis, even though their group was easier, with only Iran being the biggest opponent while the UAE and Qatar were no strangers. Bahrain lost two opening games against Iran and the UAE 0–2 and 1–2, the latter defeat was subject to the earliest goal in Asian Cup history by [[Ali Mabkhout]]. Bahrain salvaged some pride with a 2–1 win over Qatar, condemning its neighbour to bottom of the group while Bahrain finished third for the second consecutive Asian Cup.

==== 2018 World Cup and 2019 Asian Cup qualifications ====
In the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2018 FIFA World Cup qualifiers]] second round, Bahrain finished fourth in a group with [[Uzbekistan national football team|Uzbekistan]], [[North Korea national football team|North Korea]], [[Philippines national football team|Philippines]] and [[Yemen national football team|Yemen]]. The poor performance of the Bahraini side caused huge public uproar over the team's ongoing decline, rocked the chair of manager [[Sergio Batista]]. He was eventually sacked and replaced by Czech youth coach [[Miroslav Soukup]], who decided to revamp the team.

Later on, Bahrain participated in the [[2019 AFC Asian Cup qualification]], where finished first in the third round in a group with [[Turkmenistan national football team|Turkmenistan]], [[Chinese Taipei national football team|Chinese Taipei]] and [[Singapore national football team|Singapore]], to qualify to the next AFC Asian Cup. However, the team's performance was far from perfect. The team suffered a home draw to minnows Singapore, before getting humiliated by Chinese Taipei away 1–2 that was considered as a shock, since Taiwan is not a football nation. This defeat also prompted Bahrain's main star, [[Ismail Abdullatif]], to retire from the team.

Bahrain managed some promising performance during this deteriorating era, reaching semi-finals of the [[23rd Arabian Gulf Cup]] held in Kuwait.

=== Short-lived glory ===
==== 2019: Asian Cup; WAFF Championship and Gulf Cup champions ====
At the [[2019 AFC Asian Cup]], Bahrain was grouped with [[India men's national football team|India]], [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]] and hosts [[United Arab Emirates national football team|United Arab Emirates]]. The Bahrainis managed a promising early performance when they faced the hosts, scored a goal lead in 78', but was eventually held 1–1 following an unclear penalty decision by the Jordanian referee [[Adham Makhadmeh]]. However, Bahrain suffered a blasting loss to Thailand 0–1, leaving the team flounder despite its earlier performance. In the final match against India, which the Indians only required a draw to progress, Bahrain however managed to get a needed penalty in injury time, where [[Jamal Rashid]] turned hero as Bahrain won the fixture 1–0 to seal the team into the knockout stage for the only second times ever, and eliminated the Indians from the competition. The Bahraini side then played its own round of sixteen match, where they lost 2–1 to [[South Korea national football team|South Korea]] after extra time. This was considered as a major success for Bahraini football, and also to be the end of the country's football misfortune that endured since 2010s.

Afterwards, Bahrain managed to win two competitions for the first time, after defeating both [[Iraq national football team|Iraq]] and [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]], 1–0 under the leadership of [[Hélio Sousa]] against all odds, in the [[2019 WAFF Championship|WAFF Championship]] and [[24th Arabian Gulf Cup|Gulf Cup]] respectively.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bna.bh/en/BahrainwinsWAFFChampionship2019title.aspx?cms=q8FmFJgiscL2fwIzON1%2BDhB4tnDZzKFHBvE2YrE9yEY%3D|title=Bahrain wins WAFF Championship 2019 title|website=bna.bh|date=15 August 2019|access-date=10 February 2020|archive-date=15 April 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210415084918/https://www.bna.bh/en/BahrainwinsWAFFChampionship2019title.aspx?cms=q8FmFJgiscL2fwIzON1%2BDhB4tnDZzKFHBvE2YrE9yEY%3D|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://en.as.com/en/2019/12/08/football/1575817529_124447.html|title=Bahrain beat Saudi Arabia to win the 2019 Gulf Cup|website=as.com|date=8 December 2019|access-date=10 February 2020|archive-date=8 December 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191208153413/https://en.as.com/en/2019/12/08/football/1575817529_124447.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

==== 2022 World Cup ====
Bahrain defeated Iran 1–0 in the [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC second round|2022 FIFA World Cup Qualification Round 2]], delivering a major upset in the qualifying process, and with Bahrain enjoying huge edge in the qualifiers, Bahrain was expected to reach the third round. However, due to [[COVID-19 pandemic]], Bahrain's great progression in 2019 was reversed when it lost significant home supports (despite being designated as hosts for the remaining games) due to pandemic, as fans were barred from attending, Iran having replaced manager as well, combining the [[Bahraini Premier League|Bahrain's domestic league]] under frequent disruption due to the pandemic, all left Bahraini players little time to organise their team. Bahrain triumphed against [[Cambodia national football team|Cambodia]] 8–0 in their first game since the pandemic began, but against an Iranian side that was entirely revamped, a Bahraini side without home support was completely demoralised, losing 0–3 in process. This defeat proved to be disastrous for Bahrain, as their 4–0 victory over [[Hong Kong national football team|Hong Kong]]<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.news.cn/english/2021-06/16/c_1310010689.htm |title=Bahrain beat Hong Kong of China in FIFA World Cup qualifier |website=Xinhuanet |date=16 June 2021 |access-date=11 January 2023 |archive-date=11 January 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230111005443/http://www.news.cn/english/2021-06/16/c_1310010689.htm |url-status=live }}</ref> was too little, too late, due to Iran prevailing 1–0 over Iraq in the final game.

==== 2023 Asian Cup ====
In the [[2023 AFC Asian Cup]], Bahrain won their group (which contained Malaysia, South Korea, and Jordan). After a first matchday loss to South Korea, they followed it up with back-to-back wins, before falling to Japan in the Round of 16.

==== 2026 FIFA World Cup qualification ====
Bahrain's [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2026 World Cup qualifying]] campaign had been the worst since their return to the final round in 16 years. After dominating the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC second round|second round]] with ease, Bahrain started the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round|third round]] with a glorious shock 1–0 away win over Australia in the opener, triggering widespread optimism of Bahrain's fulfillment of qualifying for a maiden FIFA World Cup. Yet, what followed later had been a string of catastrophic defeats, such as consecutive losses to Japan, and three shock losses to China and Indonesia, despite being unbeaten to Australia and a goalless away draw to Saudi Arabia, and their conquest at the [[26th Arabian Gulf Cup]]. In particular, the shock 1–0 home defeat to China, as well as the similar shock away loss by the same scoreline against Indonesia, were particularly damaging as they contributed to Bahrain's WCQ collapse. More noteworthy, after winning the Gulf Cup in early January 2025, Bahrain failed to score in four consecutive WCQ fixtures while conceded six in total, suggesting Bahrain had severe issues in finding reliable strikers throughout the remainders. In the end, Bahrain finished bottom of the table with only five goals (the worst offensive power in the third round) and just two sole goalscorers, [[Mahdi Abduljabbar]] and [[Mohamed Marhoon]], to end the country's forgettable final round campaign as Bahrain's [[2026 FIFA World Cup]] quest ended in disaster as Bahrain missed out of a spot for the World Cup again.

== Coaching staff ==
=== Current coaching staff ===
{| class="wikitable"
! Role
! Name
|-
| Head coach
| {{flagicon|CRO}} [[Dragan Talajić]]
|-
| rowspan="2" | Assistant coaches
| {{flagicon|CRO}} [[Goran Lacković]]
|-
| {{flagicon|BHR}} Ahmed Kamal
|-
| Fitness Coach
| {{flagicon|CRO}} Matej Filipić
|-
| Team manager
| {{flagicon|BHR}} Mahmoud Riyad
|}

=== Coaching history ===
{{updated|March 2019}}<ref>{{cite web |title=Bahrain National Team Coaches |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/bah-coach.html |website=RSSSF |access-date=2 February 2023 |archive-date=7 January 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230107234414/https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/bah-coach.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
Caretaker managers are listed in ''italics''.
{{div col|colwidth=22em}}
*{{flagicon|BHR|1932}} [[Jassim Al-Maawada]] and <br />{{flagicon|EGY}} [[Abdul-Salam Alam]] (1966)
*{{flagicon|EGY|1972}} [[Hamada El-Sharqawi]] (1970–1974)
*{{flagicon|SCO}} [[Danny McLennan]] (1974–1975)
*{{flagicon|BHR|1972}} [[Adnan Ayoub]] and <br />{{flagicon|BHR|1972}} [[Khalifa Al-Hamdan]] (1975)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[Jack Mansell]] (1976)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[Mal Thompson]] and [[Michael Gorman (footballer)|Michael Gorman]] (1979)
*{{flagicon|YUG}} [[Ljubiša Broćić]] (1979–1981)
*{{flagicon|EGY}} [[Farouq Ahmed Ali]] (1981)
*{{flagicon|BRA|1968}} [[Sebastião Pereira de Araújo]] (1982–1984, 1992–1993)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[Keith Burkinshaw]] (1984–1986)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[Robbie Stepney]] (1986–1987)
*{{flagicon|TUN|1959}} [[Abdelmajid Chetali]] (1988)
*{{flagicon|TUN|1959}} [[Mohamed Al-Arabi Al-Zouaui]] (1988)
*{{flagicon|FRG}} [[Uli Maslo]] (1990–1992)
*{{flagicon|BHR|1972}} [[Faisal Alshehabı]] (2007)
*{{flagicon|FRY}} [[Ivan Čabrinović]] (1994)
*{{flagicon|ROU}} [[Victor Stănculescu (football manager)|Victor Stănculescu]] (1995–1996)
*{{flagicon|BHR|1972}} [[Fuad Boshqar]] (1996)
*{{flagicon|AUT}} [[Josef Hickersberger]] (1997)
*{{flagicon|BRA}} [[Ernesto Rosa Guedes]] (1998)
*{{flagicon|ROU}} [[Alexandru Moldovan]] (1999)
*{{flagicon|BHR|1972}} [[Ahmed D. Al Jalahama]] (2000)
*{{flagicon|GER}} [[Wolfgang Sidka]] (2001–2002, 2005)
*{{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Yves Herbet]] (2003)
*{{flagicon|CRO}} [[Srećko Juričić]] (2003–2005)
*{{flagicon|CRO}} [[Luka Peruzović]] (2005–2006)
*{{flagicon|BHR}} [[Riyadh Al-Thawadi]] (2006)
*{{flagicon|GER}} [[Hans-Peter Briegel]] (2006–2007)
*{{flagicon|BIH}} ''[[Senad Kreso]]'' (2007)
*{{flagicon|CZE}} [[Milan Máčala]] (2008–2010)
*{{flagicon|AUT}} [[Josef Hickersberger]] (2010)
*{{flagicon|BHR}} ''[[Marjan Eid]]'' (2010)
*{{flagicon|BHR}} [[Salman Sharida]] (2010–2011)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[Peter Taylor (footballer, born 1953)|Peter Taylor]] (2011–2012)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[Gabriel Calderón]] (2012–2013)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[Anthony Hudson (soccer)|Anthony Hudson]] (2013–2014)
*{{flagicon|IRQ}} [[Adnan Hamad]] (2014)
*{{flagicon|BHR}} [[Marjan Eid]] (2014–2015)
*{{flagicon|ARG}} [[Sergio Batista]] (2015–2016)
*{{flagicon|Czech Republic}} [[Miroslav Soukup]] (2016–2019)
*{{flagicon|POR}} [[Hélio Sousa]] (2019–2023)
*{{flagicon|ESP}} [[Juan Antonio Pizzi]] (2023–2024)
*{{flagicon|CRO}} [[Dragan Talajić]] (2024– )
{{div col end}}

== Players ==
{{update|section|reason=New squad announcement [https://www.instagram.com/p/DZCfcEzszUP/?hl=en]|date=June 2026}}

=== Current squad ===
The following players were called up for the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round|2026 FIFA World Cup qualification]] games against [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]] and [[China national football team|China]] in June 2025.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.instagram.com/p/DKJg5Yzs9Rs/?hl=en|date=27 May 2025|title=قائمة الأحمر لمواجهتي السعودية والصين ضمن الجولتين التاسعة والعاشرة من المرحلة الثالثة من تصفيات كأس العالم 2026 🇧🇭📝|publisher=Bahrain FA}}</ref>

:''Caps and goals correct as of 10 June 2025, after the match against [[China national football team|China]].''

{{nat fs g start}}
{{nat fs g player|no=1|pos=GK|name=[[Abdulkarim Fardan]]|age={{birth date and age|1992|4|25|df=y}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[Al-Riffa SC|Al-Riffa]]|clubnat=BHR}}

=== Recent call-ups ===
The following players have been called up for the team in the last 12 months.
<!--Sorted by position, most recent call-up, caps, goals and last name.-->
{{nat fs r start}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Ammar Mohamed]]|age={{birth date and age|1999|2|10|df=y}}|caps=3|goals=0|club=[[Al-Khaldiya SC|Al-Khaldiya]]|clubnat=BHR|latest=[[26th Arabian Gulf Cup]]}}

{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Mohamed Al-Banna]]|age={{birth date and age|1989|10|17|df=y}}|caps=2|goals=0|club=[[Al-Muharraq SC|Al-Muharraq]]|clubnat=BHR|latest=v. {{fb|YEM}}, 6 June 2024}}

{{nat fs break}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Abdulwahab Al-Malood]]|age={{birth date and age|1990|6|7|df=y}}|caps=86|goals=5|club=[[Al-Muharraq SC|Al-Muharraq]]|clubnat=BHR|latest=[[26th Arabian Gulf Cup]]}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Abbas Al-Asfoor]]|age={{birth date and age|1999|2|2|df=y}}|caps=16|goals=0|club=[[Al-Ahli Club (Manama)|Al-Ahli]]|clubnat=BHR|latest=[[26th Arabian Gulf Cup]]}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Moses Atede]]|age={{birth date and age|df=y|1997|12|17}}|caps=8|goals=0|club=[[Kuching City F.C.|Kuching City]]|clubnat=MAS|latest=v. {{fb|AUS}}, 19 November 2024}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Sayed Sharaf]]|age={{birth date and age|2002|12|23|df=y}}|caps=0|goals=0|club=[[Al-Ahli Club (Manama)|Al-Ahli]]|clubnat=BHR|latest=v. {{fb|KSA}}, 15 October 2024}}
{{nat fs r player|pos=MF|name=[[Mohamed Al-Hardan]]|age={{birth date and age|1997|10|6|df=y}}|caps=29|goals=2|club=[[Al-Muharraq SC|Al-Muharraq]]|clubnat=BHR|latest=v. {{fb|NEP}}, 26 March 2024}}
{{nat fs break}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Mohamed Al-Romaihi]]|age={{birth date and age|1990|9|9|df=y}}|caps=53|goals=17|club=[[Al-Khaldiya SC|Al-Khaldiya]]|clubnat=BHR|latest=v. {{fb|KSA}}, 5 June 2025}} <sup>INJ</sup>
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Hashim Sayed Isa]]|age={{birth date and age|1998|4|3|df=y}}|caps=19|goals=6|club=[[Al-Riffa SC|Al-Riffa]]|clubnat=BHR|latest=v. {{fb|KSA}}, 15 October 2024}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Abdulla Yusuf Helal]]|age={{birth date and age|1993|6|12|df=y}}|caps=89|goals=13|club=[[Bohemians 1905]]|clubnat=CZE|latest=v. {{fb|UAE}}, 11 June 2024}}
{{nat fs break}}
<sup>INJ</sup> Withdrew due to injury<br />
<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad / standby<br />
<sup>RET</sup> Retired from the national team<br />
<sup>SUS</sup> Serving suspension<br />
<sup>WD</sup> Player withdrew from the squad due to non-injury issue.
{{nat fs end}}

== Individual records ==
:{{Updated|5 June 2026}}<ref>{{cite web |last1=Mamrud |first1=Roberto |title=Bahrain - Record International Players |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/bah-recintlp.html |website=RSSSF |access-date=2 February 2023 |archive-date=4 February 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230204154600/https://rsssf.org/miscellaneous/bah-recintlp.html |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Bahrain |url=https://www.national-football-teams.com/country/16/2024/Bahrain.html |website=National Football Teams}}</ref>
:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Bahrain.''

=== Most appearances ===
[[File:Sayed Mohammed Jaffer.jpg|right|125px|thumb|Sayed Mohammed Jaffer is Bahrain's most capped player with 163 appearances.]]

{| class="wikitable sortable" style="text-align:center"

! width="150" | Name
! width="50" | Caps
! width="50" | Goals

| 6
| style="text-align:left" | '''[[Ismail Abdullatif]]'''{{efn|name=Abdullatif|Abdullatif also appeared in 6 matches that are not considered international 'A' matches by FIFA - including scoring two goals against [[Syria national under-23 football team|Syria U-23s]] and [[Jordan national under-23 football team|Jordan Olympic]] team - therefore these matches are not counted here.}}
| 138
| 47
| 2005–present
|-
| 7

=== Asian Games ===

|}

{{notelist}}

== Honours ==
=== Regional ===
* '''[[Arabian Gulf Cup]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (2)''': [[2019 Gulf Cup of Nations|2019]], [[26th Arabian Gulf Cup|2024–25]]
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (4): [[1970 Gulf Cup of Nations|1970]], [[1982 Gulf Cup of Nations|1982]], [[1992 Gulf Cup of Nations|1992]], [[2003 Gulf Cup of Nations|2003]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (3): [[1990 Gulf Cup of Nations|1990]], [[1994 Gulf Cup of Nations|1994]], [[2004 Gulf Cup of Nations|2004]]
* '''[[WAFF Championship]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (1)''': [[2019 WAFF Championship|2019]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (1): [[2014 WAFF Championship|2014]]
* '''[[Arab Games]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Gold medal (1)''': [[2011 Arab Games|2011]]
* '''[[Arab Cup]]'''
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (2): [[1985 Arab Cup|1985]], [[2002 Arab Cup|2002]]
